title: Tapping for "Am I Spoiling Them?"
subtitle: A tapping practice for releasing the fear that financial generosity with children does harm rather than good
excerpt: A five-minute tapping practice for the guilt loop that arises when giving children a financially easier life triggers the fear of spoiling them. The script works with the worry, traces it to inherited scarcity beliefs, and moves toward confident, clear generosity.
sourceNotes: Original tapping script written for homemade.education. Uses Gary Craig's EFT framework (mid-1990s). The fear of spoiling children through financial ease is explored in Rebecca J Page's MONEY (2025) and in public-domain developmental psychology literature.

practice.whenToUse: Use when giving children something financially generous triggers guilt, second-guessing, or the voice that says you are making them soft.

content.text: A five-minute tapping practice for the fear that giving children financial ease will harm rather than help them. The script traces the guilt loop back to inherited scarcity beliefs: the idea that struggle produces character, and that removing it removes something important. It works toward a clearer understanding of what generosity actually teaches.
New to tapping? Read
how EFT tapping works
first.
The practice
Karate chop
Even though I worry that giving them too much will make them soft, I deeply and completely accept myself.
Even though part of me believes struggle is what builds character, and that removing it is risky, I deeply and completely accept myself.
Even though I'm afraid that my generosity might become their limitation, I honour that fear and I am open to releasing it now.
Tapping round
Eyebrow: this fear that ease spoils them.
Side of eye: I learned that struggle meant character, and I don't want to take that from them.
Under eye: but I also don't want to pass on the scarcity that ran through my own childhood.
Under nose: the fear that they won't learn to handle hardship.
Chin: I am teaching them something by what I give. I want to know what.
Collarbone: ease teaches its own lessons — and they are not the ones I fear.
Under arm: I can give generously and still raise children who know how to handle difficulty.
Top of head: the fear of spoiling them is an old story I'm ready to look at clearly.
Reframe to positive
Eyebrow: I give and I teach. Those are not the same gesture.
Side of eye: ease is something I can give them. Character comes from how I raise them, not from what I withhold.
Under eye: I choose not to pass on the scarcity I inherited.
Under nose: I am generous, and I am intentional.
Chin: I know the difference between giving and absenting myself.
Collarbone: I give from full, not from guilt.
Under arm: my children inherit what I decide to pass on, not just what I give.
Top of head: I am breaking the cycle, not creating a new problem.
Tap each round three times, then move to the reframe and tap that three times.
Where this practice comes from
Written for homemade.education. Uses Gary Craig's EFT tapping framework, in use since the mid-1990s.
